title: Geometry and Arithmetic - Finiteness Theorems for Etale Cohomolgy of Excellent
speaker: Ofer Gabber

proof_stages.label: Excellent schemes and the finiteness theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls excellence/quasi-excellence, states the expected finiteness theorem for étale cohomology with invertible torsion coefficients—constructibility of higher direct images and eventual vanishing—and announces the replacement of resolution of singularities by alterations, approximation, Epp's theorem, and absolute purity.

proof_stages.label: Valuative h-local uniformization with ℓ-control
proof_stages.summary: The speaker formulates weak and refined local-uniformization statements in the h-topology: after generically finite alterations, closed subsets become normal-crossing divisors on regular models, with additional control of inertia or ℓ-Sylow subgroups via equivariant, Nisnevich, and finite-flat prime-to-ℓ diagrams.

proof_stages.label: Approximation from completions to excellent local rings
proof_stages.summary: Popescu/Artin approximation is strengthened using I-adic closeness, two-sided Rees rings, and spectral-sequence control of exactness so that finite-presentation geometric data over completions can be approximated while preserving properties such as reducedness, normality, dimension, and regularity.

proof_stages.label: Log-regular and very tame quotient technology
proof_stages.summary: The proof develops log-regular local structure, canonical toric/log desingularization, and blowup/enlargement procedures that turn tame finite group actions into very tame actions whose quotients remain log regular and compatible with log-smooth and nodal-curve constructions.

proof_stages.label: Purity descended through prime-to-ℓ alterations
proof_stages.summary: Gysin and trace maps for relative complete intersections are used to split purity morphisms after a generically finite alteration of degree prime to ℓ, reducing absolute cohomological purity to log-smooth standard models over complete discrete valuation rings.

proof_stages.label: Epp partial algebraization of complete local rings
proof_stages.summary: Using Epp elimination of wild ramification and henselian/formal algebraization, complete normal local rings are made finite over relative curves over bases of one lower dimension, allowing the weak local-uniformization theorem to be proved by induction with de Jong-style alterations.

proof_stages.label: Constructibility and boundedness of higher direct images
proof_stages.summary: The main finiteness theorem is deduced for open immersions first by h-cohomological descent from good hypercovers and then by a codimension induction using the refined alteration diagram to show the relevant kernels and images are bounded constructible.

proof_stages.label: Rigidified dualizing complexes and biduality
proof_stages.summary: Under the existence of a dimension function, the speaker constructs candidate dualizing complexes from prescribed local cohomology and perverse middle-extension arguments, then proves uniqueness and biduality using finite-type stability and partial algebraization.

examples.name: Non-finite normalization outside excellence
examples.note: The speaker mentions noetherian local schemes whose normalization need not be finite to illustrate the pathologies excluded by excellence, without using a particular ring in the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker only cites non-finite normalization as a phenomenon showing why excellence is needed, without constructing or analyzing such a scheme

passes.reason: Used to show finite normalization fails for general non-excellent noetherian schemes, with no sporadic, limiting, extremal, or maximal-symmetry structure.
A non-excellent scheme with non-finite normalization is used to show finiteness can fail outside excellence, but it is not structurally degenerate, extremal, symmetric, or sporadic-exceptional.

A non-excellent noetherian local scheme with non-finite normalization is cited as a failure of the usual finiteness property, not as a structurally degenerate, extremal, symmetric, or sporadic object.
Used as a pathology showing that outside excellence a Noetherian local scheme can fail finite normalization.
A non-excellent Noetherian scheme/ring with non-finite normalization is used to show finite normalization fails without excellence, but it has no listed intrinsic special structure.
A non-excellent noetherian/local scheme with non-finite normalization is used as a pathology showing normalization finiteness fails outside excellence.
A non-excellent Noetherian local scheme with non-finite normalization is cited to show the finiteness/excellence-based expectations fail outside excellence.
used to show that finite normalization can fail without excellence, but the object is not trivial, extremal, sporadic, or maximally symmetric.
A non-excellent Noetherian scheme with non-finite normalization is cited to show finiteness can fail without excellence, but it is not sporadic, symmetric, degenerate, or extremal.
a non-excellent scheme with non-finite normalization is invoked to show finite normalization fails without excellence
A non-excellent local Noetherian scheme with non-finite normalization is cited to show finiteness fails without excellence, but is not intrinsically extremal, symmetric, exceptional, or degenerate.
used to show that without excellence the expected finiteness of normalization can fail, while the scheme/ring is not intrinsically sporadic, degenerate, extremal, or maximally symmetric

examples.name: Abhyankar lemma for normal crossing divisors
examples.note: The familiar normal-crossings form of Abhyankar's lemma is invoked as an analogy for the log-regular tame-cover purity statement and its very-tame quotient conclusion.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker invokes Abhyankar’s lemma for normal crossing divisors as an analogy/known case of tame ramification, without working through the lemma on a concrete divisor

passes.reason: Invoked as the standard tame normal-crossing case of the purity/ramification statement, not as a failure case or structurally exceptional object.
The normal-crossing-divisor setting in Abhyankar’s lemma is a regular/toroidal positive model for tame ramification, not an adversarial example or classification exception.
The normal-crossing divisor case is a highly regular local coordinate-hyperplane/log-regular situation used as a positive model for tame ramification, not to refute a claim.
The normal-crossing Abhyankar lemma is invoked as the standard positive tame-ramification model, not to refute a claim.

The normal-crossing-divisor case of Abhyankar's lemma is invoked as a standard tame-ramification model, not to refute a claim and not as a sporadic, extremal, degenerate, or maximally symmetric object.
The normal-crossing Abhyankar-lemma situation is a standard tame local model used positively as an analogy, not a sporadic, extremal, symmetric, or degenerate object.

The normal-crossing divisor case of Abhyankar’s lemma is used as a standard tame/local model rather than to refute a claim, and normal crossings give a highly regular coordinate-hyperplane configuration.
invoked as a standard tame-ramification result for the highly regular coordinate-hyperplane case of normal crossing divisors, not as a refutation.
Abhyankar's lemma for normal crossing divisors is used as the standard tame-ramification model, with the divisor structurally regular like coordinate hyperplanes rather than adversarial.
the normal-crossing divisor case is used as a standard tame-ramification model for Abhyankar’s lemma, not to refute a claim
Abhyankar's lemma for normal-crossing divisors is used as the standard tame-ramification model, and normal crossings are locally coordinate-hyperplane configurations with strong regularity.

Abhyankar's lemma for tame covers along normal crossing divisors is invoked as a standard positive model/special case of log purity, not as a failure case, and normal crossings are not exceptional, degenerate, extremal, or symmetric in the specified sense
